prompt: Translate the following English sentence into French, Japanese, and Arabic, and add a brief note on any phrase that was tricky to render faithfully: "The silence between us said more than any argument could have."
response:
French: Le silence entre nous disait plus que n'aurait pu le faire une dispute. 
Note: This is quite a literal translation. "More than any argument could have" is rendered as "plus que n'aurait pu le faire une dispute", keeping the comparative and conditional tenses from the English.

Japanese: 私たちの間の沈黙は、口論が言えた以上に多くのことを物語っていた。
(Watashitachi no aida no chinmoku wa, kūron ga iteita ijō ni ōkina koto o mono kazatteita.)
Note: In Japanese, the meaning is conveyed using the verb 話す (hanasu) - to speak or tell. Literally, this translates to "The silence between us told (said) more than what a verbal dispute could have."

Arabic: الصمت بيننا كان ي-empty- أكثر مما كان للجدال أن ي-empty-
(Alसmatu bainana kan ya-empty- akthara mimma kana lil湿地aślil ana ي-empty-)
Note: Arabic does not have a perfect equivalent for "could have" in a relative clause like this. Instead, it has been rendered as ي-empty-, representing a hypothetical simultaneity. The word "empty" would be filled in with a verb to complete the Arabic sentence, such as "cannot" (لا يقدر).
